title: Creating Data Frames
description: Create data frames from vectors using data.frame(). Understand row names and column names.

slides.title: Creating with data.frame() 🛠️
slides.content:
The primary function to create a data frame is `data.frame()`. You provide named vectors (or other objects) as arguments.

```r
df <- data.frame(
  name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"),
  age = c(25, 30, 35),
  employed = c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
)
```

By default, character vectors are converted to factors (you can change this with `stringsAsFactors = FALSE`).

slides.title: Create Your First Data Frame
slides.codeSnippet:
df <- data.frame(
  id = 1:3,
  name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"),
  score = c(85, 92, 78),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
print(df)

quizData.question: What does `stringsAsFactors = FALSE` do?

options.text: Prevents character columns from being converted to factors

quizData.explanation: In older R versions, strings were converted to factors by default. Setting stringsAsFactors=FALSE keeps them as character.

slides.title: Row Names
slides.content:
Data frames automatically get row names, usually just `1,2,3,...`. You can assign custom row names with the `row.names` argument or later with `rownames()`.

```r
df <- data.frame(
  x = 1:3, 
  y = 4:6,
  row.names = c("obs1", "obs2", "obs3")
)
```

slides.title: Custom Row Names
slides.codeSnippet:
df <- data.frame(
  product = c("A", "B", "C"),
  price = c(10, 15, 20),
  row.names = c("p1", "p2", "p3")
)
print(df)
rownames(df)
